content.full_text:
June 14, 1976

Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator
Edith Schwartz, Treasurer
WOMEN FOR:
3913 West Olympic Boulevard
Beverly Hills, California 90211

Dear Ms. Cooperman and Ms. Schwartz:

This letter is in response to your letters of May 14, 1976 and
May 24, 1976, regarding the campaign disclosure reporting obli-
gations of WOMEN FOR: under the Political Reform Act.

Based on these letters and information you have previously furnished
our office, we have determined that WOMEN FOR: is not a committee
existing primarily for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or
election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure. Therefore, under the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Act, the organization is required to report only
the portion of its contributions which will be used for political
purposes. Regarding allocation of receipts, 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18215 provides, "...the payment shall be apportioned on a
reasonable basis in order to determine the amount of the contri-
bution." On the basis of the information stated in your letter,
your reporting procedures can be considerably simplified. You must
report the name and other information required by Government Code
Section 84210(g) of each person who made "contributions" to your
organization of $50 or more. To determine which persons made
contributions of $50 or more, you should calculate the percentage
of your gross income that is used to make contributions and
expenditures. Apply that percentage to the total amount received
from each member in order to determine whether that member's name
must be disclosed. In your letter of May 14, 1976, you indicated
that only one name would be required to be disclosed if this
procedure were followed for 1975. As far as reporting contributions
received during 1976, even though you cannot specifically calculate
what percentage of your total receipts will be used for political
purposes, the provisions of the Political Reform Act will be complied
with if you make a good faith estimate of the percentage that will
probably be expended to support or oppose candidates and measures

Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator
Edith Schwartz, Treasurer
WOMEN FOR: -2- June 14, 1976


during this calendar year, and base your reporting on that estimate.
The estimate could be based on the percentage from last year,
although you may want to make some adjustment if you anticipate more
political activity this year because it is an election year. Then
you may apply that percentage to determine whether any of your
members have made contributions of $50 or more. At the end of the
calendar year, if you revise the percentage based on actual receipts
and expenditures and determine that several names have been omitted,
you may disclose those names on your final statement. In addition,
you must report the names of any persons who make payments to you
that are specifically earmarked for the support or opposition of
candidates or measures. Thus, if someone knows that WOMEN FOR:
supports a certain measure and gives you $100 to campaign for that
measure, you must disclose the name of that person.

Hopefully the allocation method described above will simplify your
bookkeeping and reporting responsibilities. AB 4349 now pending
in the Legislature will, if enacted, further alleviate your bookkeeping burden. It proposes raising the disclosure threshold for
each contributor from $50 to $100.

If we can be of further assistance, please contact our staff.

Sincerely,


NAN HAMBLETON
Special Compliance Representative

women for:
continuing volunteer support and action for issues, candidates and legislation of our choice on local, state and national levels.

May 14, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Nan Hambleton, Re: Request for opinion.

Dear Sirs:

WOMEN FOR: requests definitive instruction as to compliance
with reporting all contributions of $50 or more.

We are an on-going, volunteer organization, and only part of
our activities relate to political support of candidates and
ballot issues. We raise most of our funds from membership
dues (most come in at $7.50, $10 and $15 per year) and various
special events ranging from coffees to luncheons (ticket prices
of $5 to $20.00 in 1975 and 1976) and general membership meet-
ings ($1 to $3 in price, when we charge to meet expenses).
No event is designed to raise funds for a specific candidate.
All monies go into our general fund and at election time, we
determine what we can afford to contribute, and allocate money
accordingly.

WOMEN FOR: closes its books as of December 31st of each year,
and starts anew on January 1st. For this reason, we are cur-
rently planning to submit a list of all names who in gross
contributions came to $50 or more for the year 1975, and then
another list which starts as of January 1, 1976, and goes up
to the filing date of May 24, 1976. For your information we
have filed no report since July 31, 1975, because we made no
further political contributions since then, and we're informed
we could pick up our report when next we make political contrib-
utions.

Our question to you pertains to a method of compiling this list
that is easily accomplished by our volunteer workers, while
still meeting your requirements. We believe in open and full
reporting, but wonder whether we are over-complying with the
requirement.

Once we closed our books for 1975, we could compute what percent-
age of our income went for political contributions in 1975.
/Continued...

8913 West Olympic Boulevard • Beverly Hills • California 90211 • Telephone 657-7411

COORDINATOR Roslyn Cooperman     ASSISTANT COORDINATOR Muriel Blankfort     STEERING COMMITTEE Susan Adler Mary Ahrm Myna
Anson Margaret Benkert Helen Bliss Susan Blume Phyllis Cohen Barbara Cottrell Midge Costley Kathleen Custer Barbara Davis Mary Delman

PRESIDENTS COUNCIL Mary Agress *Barbara Boyle Joan Burks     AREA COORDINATORS

womenfor:
continuing volunteer support and action for issues, candidates and legislation of our choice on local, state and national levels

Page 2

Our gross income in 1975 was $71,744.41
Our net income was $38,168.50
Our political contributions in 1975 totalled $7,924.60

The $7,924.60 figure included the cost of printing and mailing
our newsletter issue that listed the candidates we supported,
plus the ballot recommendations card we send along for membership convenience to take to the polls. We have been informed
this year (1976) that this printing and mailing cost need no
longer be included as a political contribution to be divided
up among the number of candidates and issues listed, since
we mail only to our membership and distribute otherwise only
upon request. Therefore, of the $7,924.60 listed for 1975,
only $6,050 went directly to candidates. Only $1,000 of this
went to a State candidate. All the rest went to local candidates for the Los Angeles City Council, Los Angeles School
Board and Community College Board.

However, even leaving the figure at $7,924.60, this constitutes
9% of our gross income of $71,744.41; 20.8% of our net income
of $38,168.50.

We will be submitting a list of approximately 192 names who,
in gross total in 1975, contributed $50 or more in terms of
membership dues and attendance at our various events. If we
were to be generous and say 25% of each individual's total
contribution was used towards political contributions, our
list would consist of 13 names. If we were to use the gross
income figures ($71,744.41) and the gross contribution figures
($7,924.60 or 9%) and figure it generously at 10%, then our list
would consist of one name.

Incidentally, if we total up the contributions of our 192 people who gave $50 or more, the total comes to $21,920.50 which
far exceeds the $7,924.60 we contributed to political support
of candidates in 1975.

Therefore, we respectfully request a ruling from you as to the
reporting that is appropriate for an organization such as ours.
It would obviously save us a lot of work and Xerox expense if
we could have a ruling from you that would allow us to do the
percentage process, but we can only do that fairly at the end
of our fiscal year, since we can only justify a percentage
figure once we know our total income for the year.

Thank you in advance for consideration of our request for help.

Very truly yours,

[illegible]
ROSLYN COOPERMAN
Coordinator

[illegible]
EDITH SCHWARTZ
Treasurer

RC/mc

women for:
continuing volunteer support and action for issues, candidates and legislation of our choice on local, state and national levels
MAY 23 12:03PM'76

May 24, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: I.D. # 744559.

To Whom It May Concern:

Attached are two lists of WOMEN FOR: contributors of $50
or more in gross total. We have filed no report since the
July 1975 one because we made no political contributions
between then and now, the current reporting period.

One list details the 1975 contributions.

The second list details contributions from January 1, 1976
to May 24, 1976.

Please note the disparity between the amount we actually
spent for political contributions and the gross total of
these $50 or more contributors. You can understand why we
have requested an FPPC opinion as to the appropriate method
of reporting contributions for an organization such as ours
which is an on-going, volunteer group that engages in many
other activities besides support of candidates and issues.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

Roslyn Cooperman
ROSLYN COOPERMAN
Coordinator

Edith Schwartz
EDITH SCHWARTZ
Treasurer

sections.question_synthetic: What are the campaign disclosure reporting obligations for WOMEN FOR: under the Political Reform Act, and how should the organization allocate and report contributions when only a portion of funds are used for political purposes?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: WOMEN FOR: is not a political committee but must report contributions of $50 or more that will be used for political purposes. The organization should calculate the percentage of gross income used for political activity and apply that percentage to determine which donors exceeded the $50 threshold, using either actual percentages from the prior year or good faith estimates for the current year, with adjustments allowed at year-end based on actual expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains advice letter from Nan Hambleton followed by extensive donor list with names, addresses, and contribution amounts. Primary legal guidance is in the letter itself; donor list appears to be supplemental documentation. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present in traditional format.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the campaign disclosure reporting obligations for WOMEN FOR: under the Political Reform Act, and how should the organization allocate and report contributions when only a portion of funds are used for political purposes?

CONCLUSION: WOMEN FOR: is not a political committee but must report contributions of $50 or more that will be used for political purposes. The organization should calculate the percentage of gross income used for political activity and apply that percentage to determine which donors exceeded the $50 threshold, using either actual percentages from the prior year or good faith estimates for the current year, with adjustments allowed at year-end based on actual expenditures.
embedding.first_500_words: June 14, 1976 Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator Edith Schwartz, Treasurer WOMEN FOR: 8913 West Olympic Boulevard Beverly Hills, California 90211 Dear Ms. Cooperman and Ms. Schwartz: This letter is in response to your letters of May 14, 1976 and May 24, 1976, regarding the campaign disclosure reporting obligations of WOMEN FOR: under the Political Reform Act. Based on these letters and information you have previously furnished our office, we have determined that WOMEN FOR: is not a committee existing primarily for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure. Therefore, under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Act, the organization is required to report only the portion of its contributions which will be used for political purposes. Regarding allocation of receipts, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 provides, "...the payment shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to determine the amount of the contribution." On the basis of the information stated in your letter, your reporting procedures can be considerably simplified. You must report the name and other information required by Government Code Section 84210(g) of each person who made "contributions" to your organization of $50 or more. To determine which persons made contributions of $50 or more, you should calculate the percentage of your gross income that is used to make contributions and expenditures. Apply that percentage to the total amount received from each member in order to determine whether that member's name must be disclosed. In your letter of May 14, 1976, you indicated that only one name would be required to be disclosed if this procedure were followed for 1975. As far as reporting contributions received during 1976, even though you cannot specifically calculate what percentage of your total receipts will be used for political purposes, the provisions of the Political Reform Act will be complied with if you make a good faith estimate of the percentage that will probably be expended to support or oppose candidates and measures during this calendar year, and base your reporting on that estimate. The estimate could be based on the percentage from last year, although you may want to make some adjustment if you anticipate more political activity this year because it is an election year. Then you may apply that percentage to determine whether any of your members have made contributions of $50 or more. At the end of the calendar year, if you revise the percentage based on actual receipts and expenditures and determine that several names have been omitted, you may disclose those names on your final statement. In addition, you must report the names of any persons who make payments to you that are specifically earmarked for the support or opposition of candidates or measures. Thus, if someone knows that WOMEN FOR: supports a certain measure and gives you $100 to campaign for that measure, you must disclose the name of
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing WOMEN FOR:'s campaign disclosure obligations, clarifying that the organization must report only the political portion of contributions and providing a simplified allocation methodology based on percentage of income used for political purposes.
